ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for donburi; 'rice dish' (FOODON_03302050) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Rice-based dishes) is the closest parent; no donburi-specific FoodEx2 code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for donburi or Japanese rice bowl as a food concept.

main_ingredients: protein topping (e.g. chicken, beef, pork, seafood, or egg)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard donburi bowl served in a Japanese restaurant typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 200 g of seasoned protein topping with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken and Egg Rice Bowl (Oyakodon) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods for steamed white rice and generic Japanese-style simmered chicken
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Donburi is a category of Japanese rice bowl dishes. No single USDA or HPB entry covers the generic 'don' category. Values are estimated as a weighted composite of steamed white rice (approx. 50% by weight, USDA FDC #169756) and a representative simmered chicken-and-egg topping (approx. 50% by weight, USDA FDC #174608 chicken thigh, cooked, with soy-mirin sauce adjustment). This represents a mid-range donburi (oyakodon / chicken don) as the most common variant. Values are per 100 g of the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled, Japanese-style) GI ~72; donburi estimated from white rice base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for generic donburi. GI of 72 is taken from boiled Japanese white rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care), which constitutes approximately 50% of the dish by weight and is the primary glycemic driver. The protein and fat topping moderates the overall meal GI slightly; however, the rice base dominates and the dish GI is conservatively estimated at 72 (high). Glycemic load per serving = (72 × 21.5 × 4.0) / 100 = 61.9 g GL for a 400 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and dashi broth
hypertension.guidance: A standard donburi bowl (400 g) contributes approximately 1280 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index white rice base driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base carries a GI of approximately 72 and a glycemic load of ~62 per standard bowl, making donburi a high-glycemic meal that requires careful portion control or substitution with lower-GI rice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium from seasoning sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Donburi provides moderate saturated fat (~3.6 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~320 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean protein toppings such as fish or tofu and request low-sodium sauce to reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Donburi (丼, どんぶり), commonly abbreviated as 'don', is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of steamed short-grain white rice served in a deep bowl and topped with a seasoned protein and sauce. The word 'donburi' refers both to the bowl itself and to the dish. Common variants include oyakodon (chicken and egg), gyudon (beef), katsudon (breaded pork cutlet), tendon (tempura), and unadon (grilled eel). The topping is typically simmered in a sweet-savoury sauce made from dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sake, giving the dish a rich umami flavour with a slightly sweet finish. The rice absorbs the sauce, creating a cohesive, satisfying texture. Donburi is a staple of Japanese home cooking and casual restaurant dining, and is widely available across Singapore in Japanese bento and izakaya-style restaurants. Nutritionally, donburi is a carbohydrate-dominant mixed dish; the white rice base provides rapid-digesting starch with a high glycemic index (~72), while the protein topping contributes moderate protein (~7–10 g per 100 g) and micronutrients including B vitamins, iron, and zinc. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce seasoning (~320 mg per 100 g), making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for gluten-free diets due to soy sauce containing wheat.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a don bowl?
What is the glycemic index of donburi?

confidence_reason: Donburi is a category of dishes rather than a single standardised recipe. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for steamed white rice and cooked chicken thigh with soy-mirin sauce, representing the most common variant (chicken don / oyakodon). No single approved primary source provides a direct 'generic donburi' nutrient profile. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled Japanese white rice. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Donburi (丼) is a dish category; this record represents a mid-range composite estimate based on a chicken-and-egg (oyakodon) variant as the most common type served in Singapore Japanese restaurants. Nutrient values are estimated composites from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (#169756 cooked white rice; #174608 chicken thigh cooked) with soy-mirin sauce adjustment; not an exact match to any single approved source entry. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as this value is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source database for this composite dish preparation. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as this value is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source database for this composite dish preparation. selenium_mcg is -1 as this value is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source database for this composite dish preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0, confirmed zero/trace in the source ingredients for this preparation per USDA FoodData Central. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from boiled Japanese white rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care); no direct donburi GI measurement exists in the approved source database. Halal status is marked false as standard donburi sauce contains mirin (alcohol) and the protein source varies; halal-certified variants exist but cannot be assumed for the generic record. The food_id GD000764 is preserved from the input seed record.
